text: Code refactoring is an important practice in software development. It means improving the internal structure of code without changing what it does. The goal is to make the code cleaner, easier to read, and simpler to maintain. For example, you might rename confusing variables, break long functions into smaller ones, or remove duplicate code. Refactoring does not add new features or fix bugs—it focuses only on quality. Over time, code can become messy if we take shortcuts. This is called "technical debt." Regular refactoring helps pay off this debt, making future work faster and less stressful. Clean code saves time and helps teams collaborate better.
